segments.title: Rebuilding the Caribbean squadron after pirate losses
segments.summary: A damaged British force in the Caribbean is rebuilt around third rates before returning to eliminate pirate bases.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke reviews a campaign in which pirate successes have made the Caribbean a naval problem rather than a minor nuisance. He explains his plan to rebuild the home fleet, send replacements from Portsmouth, and use a small concentration of third rates to regain control. The moment is useful for seeing how sea power in the game links fleet strength, bases, transport, and the security of colonial operations.

evidence.note: He describes pirate losses, the Caribbean force, shipbuilding at Portsmouth, and a force of third rates preparing to attack.

segments.title: Choosing when a naval battle needs hands-on command
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke sets a practical rule for manual naval battles and tests how a line of ships behaves with the wind.
segments.body: He distinguishes engagements worth fighting directly from those he will resolve automatically, while keeping discretion for unusual single-ship encounters. In the battle that follows, he considers wind direction, formation, and the value of taking prizes rather than simply destroying them. His frustration when attempted captures end in explosions also points to the difference between the game's outcomes and historical incentives to preserve a useful ship.

evidence.note: He states his rule for fighting naval battles, discusses wind and formation, and explains his wish to capture an enemy third rate.

segments.title: Using warships as shock forces in the game
segments.summary: After the action, Dr. Clarke compares the game's ships to cavalry: decisive early impact matters more than prolonged staying power.
segments.body: Reviewing the result, he describes the game's naval units as analogous to cavalry rather than durable line infantry. The comparison highlights the importance of the first movement and volley in this particular combat model. It is a compact watch point for viewers interested in how he is adapting land-force instincts to Empire: Total War's naval controls.

evidence.note: He says the ships must be treated like cavalry, with their effect concentrated in the initial movement and volley.

segments.title: Turning captured ships into fleet reinforcements
segments.summary: Rather than take prize money, Dr. Clarke absorbs captured ships into his force and uses them to support further operations.
segments.body: The victory is translated immediately into additional naval capability: captured ships join the British fleet instead of becoming cash. He weighs a captured carronade frigate against the immediate need for more line-of-battle ships and ties the enlarged force to reclaiming a threatened region. This is a useful game-level illustration of why prizes can matter as operational assets, not just as a reward for winning.

evidence.note: He chooses captured ships over prize money, counts the resulting third rates, and explains the fleet's next task.

segments.title: Keeping a fleet ready for Spanish operations
segments.summary: A comparison of two British squadrons shows how ship numbers and geography shape a campaign against Spain.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke inventories his forces in two waters, comparing a squadron of five third rates and a fifth rate with another built around four third rates, a fifth rate, and supporting vessels. He then connects this naval balance to planned operations against Spain and comments on the game's compressed treatment of Gibraltar and neighbouring ports. The passage is useful for following the relationship between local sea control, reinforcement, and the map's strategic geography.

evidence.note: He lists the composition of two fleets, sends reinforcements south, and discusses Gibraltar, Cadiz, and map scale.

segments.title: Breaking infantry squares with artillery and infantry
segments.summary: A brief combined-arms lesson explains why infantry squares are for stopping cavalry, and how artillery and infantry can exploit them.
segments.body: When the opposing force forms square, Dr. Clarke argues that it has chosen the wrong answer to an infantry threat. He lays out a sequence: artillery disrupts the dense formation, infantry attacks while it is broken up, and cavalry pursues a defeated enemy. Although this is a land-battle moment, it captures the tactical reasoning that guides the rest of the campaign.

evidence.note: He explains square formation, artillery's role in breaking it, infantry attack, and cavalry pursuit.

segments.title: Fixing the enemy with infantry before a cavalry flank attack
segments.summary: The planned attack uses infantry to hold the enemy's attention while cavalry descends on its rear from a hill.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes a deliberately two-directional assault: infantry gathers to the front while cavalry moves into position out of sight. The cavalry is intended to come down the hill after the enemy is committed, striking from behind rather than charging an unprepared line head-on. The moment gives a clear, concise example of fixing an opponent before using mobility to create a flank or rear attack.

evidence.note: He identifies the infantry attack direction and describes cavalry descending from a hill into the enemy's rear.
